context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m stuck on this reflection about “development as freedom” and rights-based development planning. I kind of get the phrases separately, but when I try to answer what the “added value” is or whether it helps planning, it turns into vague generalities.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — identifying vagueness early is useful. Let's build from the ground up. Before we talk about 'added value,' what do YOU think 'development as freedom' actually means? Try to put it in your own words, even if it feels rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their reflection and has identified a genuine conceptual difficulty: connecting 'development as freedom' to its practical implications for planning. Rather than explaining the concept, I should use a Socratic question to get them to activate what they already know and build from there. I'll ask them to define what they understand by 'development as freedom' in their own words first, since you can't assess added value without first having a clear grip on what the concept actually means.

exchanges.student: Honestly I’m not sure, and when I try to define it I just end up saying “development helps people live better,” which feels too broad. Could you maybe just explain the whole idea clearly first, and maybe how it connects to rights-based planning too?
exchanges.tutor: I won't explain it for you — that's your job as the writer! But your attempt isn't as bad as you think. 'Living better' is a start. Here's a push: better *how*, exactly? What are the different things a person might need in order to truly live 'better'? Try listing a few concrete examples.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the concept directly, which would violate the core rule of never giving the answer directly. Instead, I should redirect them using Socratic questioning. Their attempt ('development helps people live better') is actually not a bad starting point - I should acknowledge it has merit and push them to sharpen it rather than dismiss it. I'll ask a targeted question to help them get more specific about what 'better' means in this context.

exchanges.student: Maybe like income, education, health, safety, political voice, and having actual choices? I’m still not seeing how to turn that into an argument though, and that didn’t really get me unstuck, so I’m trying a different angle: could you just give me a big-picture explanation of what the paper is probably looking for?
exchanges.tutor: I can't give you a roadmap for what the paper 'wants' — that would be doing your thinking for you. But look at your own list: income, education, health, safety, political voice, choices. What do those things have in common? What's the *category* they all fall into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good list but is trying to redirect me into giving them the answer through a different route ('big-picture explanation of what the paper is looking for'). I should refuse this redirect clearly but kindly, and instead show them that their list is actually the key to unlocking the concept. The list they gave maps almost directly onto Sen's capabilities approach. I should push them to notice the pattern in their own list rather than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: I guess they’re all conditions that let people actually act, not just survive. But then I get lost again because I can argue both sides about planning—like maybe this makes planning better because it’s more humane, but also worse because it gets too broad. I’m kind of going in circles.
exchanges.tutor: 'Conditions that let people actually act' — that's a sharp insight, hold onto it. And the tension you're describing — more humane vs. too broad — isn't you going in circles. That *is* the argument. So here's the question: does one side of that tension outweigh the other for you, or do they depend on context? What would tip the balance?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a real conceptual breakthrough — 'conditions that let people actually act' is essentially the capabilities idea. I should affirm this briefly and not let it pass unnoticed. They're also now genuinely engaging with the planning question and seeing real tension, which is exactly what a good reflection needs. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should push them to see that the tension itself might be the argument. I'll ask them to think about whether a tension is a problem or actually the substance of a good reflection.

exchanges.student: I think it depends on context, but that feels like a cop-out unless I can make it sound sophisticated. I’m also not sure how the rights-based part fits without me just repeating “people have rights” over and over.
exchanges.tutor: Context isn't a cop-out if you specify *which* context and *why* it matters — that's what makes it sophisticated. But let's tackle the rights question: think about your list again — conditions that let people act. What does calling those things *rights* add that simply calling them *freedoms* or *capabilities* doesn't? What changes when something becomes a right?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but has two related worries: (1) 'it depends on context' feeling weak, and (2) not knowing how to bring in rights-based planning without being repetitive. These are actually connected — context is what makes rights-based planning either useful or limited. I should push them to think about what 'rights' adds that 'freedom' or 'capabilities' doesn't already cover, which will help them see the relationship rather than treating them as separate ideas.

exchanges.student: Maybe rights make it more political and enforceable, like they create duties for the state instead of just ideals. I still feel like I’m patching together fragments though, and I’d really help if I could see one compact model answer or at least a full sample paragraph.
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide a sample paragraph — that's your writing to do. But notice what you've built: development = conditions for action, rights = those conditions become state duties, tension = more humane but potentially too broad. That's not fragments, that's an argument arc. Try writing one rough paragraph using just those three ideas. Don't worry about polish — just get it down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made another strong conceptual leap — 'rights create duties for the state' is exactly the key distinction between capabilities and rights-based approaches. I must firmly refuse the request for a model answer or sample paragraph, as that would violate the core rules. However, I should acknowledge the real progress they've made and point out that they actually already have the building blocks of a coherent argument. This is a good moment to push them to start integrating what they've discovered into actual writing.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m pretty sure it’ll sound messy and generic. Before I do, can you at least tell me who “gets to exercise the right to development,” because that part is confusing me most?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually in your prompt — it's yours to answer, not mine. But you can reason it out: you just said rights create duties for the state. So who is the state obligated *to*? And does everyone have equal access to that obligation in practice? Sit with that, then write your paragraph — messy is fine for a first attempt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling before writing by asking me to answer one of the prompt's core questions directly. I should refuse but recognize this is a legitimate question from the prompt — it's not a detour. However, answering it for them would rob them of the thinking. I should push them to reason it out themselves using what they've already established: if rights create duties for the state, then who holds those rights? This should be a short, targeted Socratic nudge, and I should also gently hold them to the writing task.

exchanges.student: I think the state is obligated to citizens, but maybe also residents or communities, and in practice definitely not everyone can claim it equally. That still feels huge, and I’m not sure whether the “right to development” belongs to individuals, groups, or whole nations.
exchanges.tutor: That individual vs. collective tension is real and worth including in your reflection. But you now have enough to write — you've been building this argument for several exchanges. Stop expanding and start drafting. Write a rough paragraph on any one part of the prompt. Messy is fine. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely grappling with a complex question — individual vs. collective rights to development — which is substantive and relevant to the prompt. However, we've now had several exchanges building up concepts without the student attempting any writing. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, this is the moment to firmly redirect them to start writing. They have enough to work with. I should briefly validate their insight about the individual/collective tension (it's a real and important one) but insist they now integrate everything into a draft paragraph rather than continuing to conceptually expand.

exchanges.student: My first sentence might be: “Thinking about development as freedom adds value because it shifts attention from economic growth alone to the real opportunities people have to live the lives they value.” I’m still tempted to ask for a fuller explanation of the individual-versus-collective part, because that’s the piece I feel least solid on.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening sentence — it's specific and does real work. On the individual-collective question: you already articulated it well a moment ago. The way to get 'solid' on it isn't more explanation from me, it's writing through it. What's your next sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong opening sentence that captures the core of Sen's capabilities approach well. I should affirm this clearly without over-praising. They're trying to stall again by asking for an explanation of the individual-collective distinction, but they've already demonstrated they understand it (citizens vs. residents vs. communities, unequal access). I should acknowledge the sentence, briefly push them to keep writing rather than expanding conceptually, and remind them that their uncertainty about individual vs. collective is something they can work through in their draft itself.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “This can improve planning by making it more attentive to health, education, voice, and security, but it can also complicate planning because these goals are broad and hard to measure.” I’m still not confident that I’m not missing some major theoretical point.
exchanges.tutor: Two solid sentences with real argumentative content. You won't cover every theoretical angle in 800 words — that's fine. But look at your prompt: you've started on 'added value' and 'helps or hinders.' You still need to connect to rights-based planning and the 'who gets to exercise the right' question. How does your next sentence bridge toward one of those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced two solid sentences that together form the backbone of a good reflection. They're showing genuine progress. The worry about 'missing a major theoretical point' is somewhat natural anxiety but also risks becoming a stalling tactic. I should affirm what they have, be honest that there's always more theory but they have the essentials, and push them toward the rights-based and 'who gets to exercise the right' parts of the prompt, which they haven't yet addressed in their draft. We're also near the end of our planned conversation length, so I should encourage them to keep building momentum.
